author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Three Things Good and Laudable in Matrimony.
title: Three Things Good and Laudable in Matrimony.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 39 [XXXIV.]—Three Things Good and Laudable in Matrimony.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Marriage, therefore, is a good in all the things which are proper to the married state.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: And these are three: it is the ordained means of procreation, it is the guarantee of chastity, it is the bond of union.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: In respect of its ordination for generation the Scripture says, “I will therefore that the younger women marry, bear children, guide the house;” as regards its guaranteeing chastity, it is said of it, “The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband; and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife;” and considered as the bond of union: “What God hath joined together, let not man put asunder.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Touching these points, we do not forget that we have treated at sufficient length, with whatever ability the Lord has given us, in other works of ours, which are not unknown to you.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: In relation to them all the Scripture has this general praise: “Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For, inasmuch as the wedded state is good, insomuch does it produce a very large amount of good in respect of the evil of concupiscence; for it is not lust, but reason, which makes a good use of concupiscence.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now lust lies in that law of the “disobedient” members which the apostle notes as “warring against the law of the mind;” whereas reason lies in that law of the wedded state which makes good use of concupiscence.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: If, however, it were impossible for any good to arise out of evil, God could not create man out of the embraces of adultery.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: As, therefore, the damnable evil of adultery, whenever man is born in it, is not chargeable on God, who certainly amidst man’s evil work actually produces a good work; so, likewise, all which causes shame in that rebellion of the members which brought the accusing blush on those who after their sin covered these members with the fig-tree leaves, is not laid to the charge of marriage, by virtue of which the conjugal embrace is not only allowable, but is even useful and honourable; but it is imputable to the sin of that disobedience which was followed by the penalty of man’s finding his own members emulating against himself that very disobedience which he had practised against God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Then, abashed at their action, since they moved no more at the bidding of his rational will, but at their own arbitrary choice as it were, instigated by lust, he devised the covering which should conceal such of them as he judged to be worthy of shame.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For man, as the handiwork of God, deserved not confusion of face; nor were the members which it seemed fit to the Creator to form and appoint by any means designed to bring the blush to the creature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Accordingly, that simple nudity was displeasing neither to God nor to man: there was nothing to be ashamed of, because nothing at first accrued which deserved punishment.
